title: Google Staff Culture — Python Edition
description: Google's engineering culture as it applies to Python engineers: readability process, code review standards, SRE, OKRs, promotion, and writing code at scale.

sections.title: 1. Readability — Google's Code Review Standard
sections.description:
Google's readability process certifies that an engineer can write idiomatic, maintainable code in a language. For Python, this means knowing PEP 8 cold, understanding when to break it, and writing code that the next engineer can understand without comments.

Key standards: Google Python Style Guide extends PEP 8. pytype (Google's type checker) or mypy are required. Type annotations on ALL function signatures are mandatory for production code. pylint --rcfile=google.rc enforces the style.

What readability reviewers look for: (1) Clear names — no abbreviations. (2) Appropriate abstractions — not too many, not too few. (3) Tests that cover behavior, not implementation. (4) Type annotations that are correct, not Any. (5) Comments explaining WHY, not WHAT.

FAANG angle: 'How is code review different at Google vs a startup?' — At Google, every CL (changelist) needs a readability-approved reviewer for the language. At startup, you ship fast. Both can be correct in context.

sections.title: 2. SRE Principles — Writing Production-Ready Python
sections.description:
Google's Site Reliability Engineering culture means every Python service must be: (1) observable (logging, metrics, traces), (2) reliable (error budgets, SLIs/SLOs), (3) operable (graceful shutdown, health checks, rate limiting).

Four golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, saturation. Every Python service should expose them via Prometheus metrics or Google's Cloud Monitoring.

Error budget: 99.9% SLO means 8.76 hours of downtime per year. Spend it deliberately (deployments, experiments).

FAANG angle: 'Your Python service has p99 latency spikes after deployments. What do you do?' — (1) Check if it's a cold-start issue (warmup requests). (2) Check if dependencies are overwhelmed (circuit breakers). (3) Roll back if error budget is burning too fast.

sections.title: 3. OKRs and Impact — Framing Python Work
sections.description:
Google uses OKRs (Objectives and Key Results) to align work with impact. For Python engineers, your OKR might be: Objective: Improve ML training pipeline efficiency. KR1: Reduce training time by 30% (from 4h to 2.8h). KR2: Reduce pipeline failure rate from 5% to 1%. KR3: Add type annotations to 100% of pipeline code.

Writing good OKRs: KRs are measurable, not binary. 'Improve performance' is an objective; 'Reduce p99 latency from 200ms to 100ms' is a key result.

Impact vs effort: At Staff+ level, you choose the highest-impact project. Use ICE (Impact, Confidence, Ease) or RICE scoring.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Your team has 10 Python services with 90% test coverage but 0% type annotations. What's your OKR?' — 'Increase type coverage from 0% to 100% across all services'. Key results: (1) Migrate service A (mypy --strict clean). (2) Write team typing guide. (3) Add CI gate for typing on new code.
sections.codeBlock:
# OKR Planning Framework

# Example: Q1 Python Upgrade OKR
#
# Objective: Modernize the Python codebase for velocity and safety
#
# Key Results:
# KR1: Migrate from Python 3.8 to 3.12 across all 25 services
#     Metric: % of services on 3.12
#     Target: 100%
#
# KR2: Enable mypy --strict on all production Python code
#     Metric: type coverage % (mypy --strict-clean modules)
#     Target: 80%
#
# KR3: Reduce CI pipeline time for Python services by 50%
#     Metric: p50 CI time
#     Target: from 12min to 6min
#
# KR4: Onboard 3 team members to Python readability
#     Metric: number of readability approvals granted
#     Target: 3

# Impact scoring for project selection
# ICE score = Impact (1-10) x Confidence (1-10) x Ease (1-10)
#
# Project A: Add type hints
#   Impact: 7 (catches bugs, improves dev speed)
#   Confidence: 9 (proven at Google)
#   Ease: 5 (100K lines to annotate)
#   ICE: 315
#
# Project B: Rewrite in Rust
#   Impact: 8 (performance)
#   Confidence: 3 (team has no Rust experience)
#   Ease: 2 (huge rewrite)
#   ICE: 48

sections.title: 4. Promotion — From L5 to L6+ at FAANG
sections.description:
Promotion at FAANG is about scope, not code volume. L5 (Senior): you complete well-defined projects. L6 (Staff): you define the projects, spot the problem before being asked. L7 (Senior Staff): your impact spans the org.

L5 to L6 signals: (1) Identified a problem that nobody asked you to solve. (2) Designed a solution that works across multiple teams. (3) Unblocked other teams. (4) Mentored juniors to L4. (5) Wrote design docs that survived without revision.

Promotion packet components: (1) Self-assessment (2 paragraphs — what you did, why it matters). (2) Impact summary (quantified: 'reduced serving cost by 30%'). (3) Artifacts (design docs, CLs, launch announcements). (4) Peer reviews (3-5).

Python-specific signals: Leading a Python readability effort, building shared Python libraries used by multiple teams, writing a PEP or contributing to CPython, giving a tech talk on Python patterns.
sections.codeBlock:
# Promotion packet template
#
# Title: Led Python Modernization Across 12 Services
#
# Summary:
# Identified that our Python 3.8 codebase was blocking the team from
# using 3.12 features (match statements, improved error messages,
# faster CPython). Led a 3-month migration of 12 services from 3.8
# to 3.12 with zero downtime.
#
# Impact:
# - Reduced p50 latency by 15% (improved CPython performance)
# - Reduced production bugs by 20% (type annotations)
# - Unblocked the 3 dependent teams waiting on 3.12 features
# - Served as Python readability reviewer for 6 CLs
#
# Artifacts:
# - Design doc: go/python-312-migration
# - Migration tool: github.com/team/py-upgrader
# - Launch announcement: team@ email, 12 services migrated
#
# Peer reviews must demonstrate:
# - Technical leadership (chose the right approach)
# - Collaboration (coordinated across 12 teams)
# - Impact (measured and communicated)

sections.title: 5. Staff+ Patterns — Design Docs, Technical Vision, and Mentorship
sections.description:
At Staff+ level, your primary output is not code — it's technical direction and team enablement.

Design docs: 1-2 page documents answering: (1) What problem are we solving? (2) Why is this important now? (3) What are the options? (4) Which option did we choose and why? (5) What is the rollout plan? (6) How do we measure success?

Technical vision: A 6-12 month roadmap. 'By Q4, all our Python services will be on 3.12 with mypy --strict, 80% test coverage, and <100ms p99 latency.'

Mentorship: Staff+ engineers grow the team. Weekly 1:1s, CL reviews with detailed feedback, tech talks, and creating learning materials (like this curriculum).

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'How do you balance writing code vs unblocking the team?' — At Staff+, you write code that is leveraged (libraries, tools, frameworks, infrastructure). You spend 30-50% of time on code, and 50-70% on design, review, and mentorship.
sections.codeBlock:
# Staff+ weekly time allocation
#
# Activity                Time    Output
# Code (leverage):        35%     Shared libraries, CI tooling, migration scripts
# Design docs:            20%     go/py-migration, go/typing-strategy
# Code review:            15%     10-15 CLs/week, detailed feedback
# Mentorship:             15%     1:1s (3 people), tech talks
# Strategy/Planning:      10%     OKR setting, roadmap, cross-team syncs
# Learning:               5%      PEPs, conference talks, research
#
# Leverage = the product of (people impacted) x (time saved per person)
# A library used by 100 engineers that saves 1h/week = 100 hours/week leverage

# Design doc template concept (see design-docs.json for full template)
#
# Title: Python 3.12 Migration Design Doc
# Status: [Draft | Review | Approved]
# Author: [Name]
# Date: [Date]
#
# Context: [Current state, problem statement]
# Options: [List 2-4 approaches with tradeoffs]
# Recommendation: [Which option, with rationale]
# Rollout Plan: [Phased migration steps]
# Success Metrics: [How we'll know it worked]
# Risks: [What could go wrong and mitigations]

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Google Python Style Guide
  • Site Reliability Engineering by Beyer, Jones, Petoff, Murphy
  • The Staff Engineer's Path by Tanya Reilly
  • An Elegant Puzzle by Will Larson
  • Google's OKR Playbook — rework.co/okrs

details:
Google Python engineer workflow: (1) Write a design doc for any non-trivial project. (2) Prototype in Python with full type annotations. (3) Get readability review before submitting. (4) Write tests (unit + integration). (5) Deploy gradually (canary -> 25% -> 50% -> 100%). (6) Monitor the four golden signals. (7) Write a postmortem if anything goes wrong.

Common mistakes: Not writing a design doc (wastes time building the wrong thing); not getting readability review (code becomes unmaintainable); not tracking SLOs (no objective measure of reliability); optimizing for lines of code written instead of impact.

Staff+ expectations: Define technical strategy, unblock teams, write leveraged code, mentor juniors, and communicate impact clearly in promotion packets. Know that 70% of your impact comes from 30% of your effort — choose wisely.
